title: Court reporters and simultaneous captioners
rationale: This occupation’s core output is fully digital text generated from speech, and AI speech recognition, real-time transcription, and captioning tools are improving rapidly and already automate large portions of the work. Human workers still matter for legal-grade accuracy, speaker identification, clarification, and quality control in high-stakes settings, but the occupation is likely to be heavily reshaped by AI through automation and reduced labor needed per transcript.

title: Cashiers
rationale: Cashiers have significant AI exposure because much of the core job is routine transaction processing that can be automated through self-checkout, computer vision, digital payments, and retail automation systems. The role still requires physical presence and customer interaction, which prevents maximum exposure, but AI and adjacent automation are likely to keep reducing headcount and reshaping the job substantially.

title: Medical transcriptionists
rationale: This job is almost entirely digital, consisting of converting, editing, and entering dictated medical information into standardized records on a computer. AI speech recognition and natural language processing already perform much of the core work today, with humans increasingly limited to review and error correction, which is why the occupation is already declining.

title: Interpreters and translators
rationale: This occupation is highly exposed because a large share of the work—especially written translation and localization—is fully digital, and AI translation tools already automate or heavily assist core tasks through machine translation and post-editing. Exposure is somewhat lower than a 9 or 10 because live interpreting, legal/medical nuance, sign language, and high-stakes cultural judgment still require substantial human accuracy, presence, and trust.

rationale: Mining and geological engineers do substantial analytical and design work on computers—modeling mines, evaluating sites, monitoring production, and writing technical reports—so AI can assist meaningfully with planning, analysis, and documentation. title: Air traffic controllers
rationale: Air traffic control involves substantial information processing on screens, and AI can assist with routing, conflict detection, weather integration, and decision support—potentially letting each controller manage more traffic. But the core job is real-time, safety-critical control of aircraft in the physical world under heavy regulation, where human judgment, accountability, and rapid coordination remain major barriers to full automation.

title: Customer service representatives
rationale: This job is highly exposed because much of the core work—answering questions, handling routine requests, processing account changes, documenting interactions, and communicating by phone/chat/email—is digital and increasingly automatable by AI. Some roles still require human judgment, empathy, or in-person help for complex and upset customers, but the occupation is already being reduced by self-service systems and AI will likely further shrink headcount by handling a growing share of standard interactions.

title: Secretaries and administrative assistants
rationale: This occupation is heavily exposed because much of the core work is routine digital information processing: scheduling, document preparation, email and phone handling, database maintenance, transcription, and basic bookkeeping. AI can automate or greatly accelerate many of these tasks, and even where full replacement is limited by coordination or interpersonal needs, each worker can support more people, reducing headcount demand.

title: Tellers
rationale: Tellers’ work includes highly routine, rules-based transaction processing and customer service tasks that are increasingly being automated through ATMs, kiosks, online banking, fraud detection, and remote service tools. The job still requires physical presence to handle cash and interact with customers, but AI and related automation can significantly reduce headcount by shifting routine transactions away from human tellers and making each remaining teller more productive.

rationale: This occupation is highly exposed because many core tasks—data entry, document preparation, filing, scheduling, answering routine inquiries, and processing office paperwork—are routine information-processing activities that AI and related software can automate or greatly accelerate. Some in-person duties remain, such as receiving visitors and handling physical mail, but the role is still likely to be heavily restructured and reduced as AI boosts productivity and shifts clerical work to other employees.

title: Construction and building inspectors
rationale: This job’s core work is physical and site-based: inspecting structures, using testing equipment, climbing through spaces, and verifying real-world conditions against code. AI can help with plan review, report writing, code lookup, scheduling, and some remote inspection workflows, but it cannot replace most onsite inspection duties, so exposure is moderate rather than high.

title: Announcers and DJs
rationale: This occupation is split between highly exposed broadcast/podcast work and lower-exposure live event DJ/emcee work. Broadcast announcing is largely digital—researching, writing, recording, editing, and even presenting scripted content—and BLS explicitly notes stations may replace live DJs with AI, but live audience interaction, on-air personality, interviewing, and in-person event hosting still preserve meaningful human value.

title: Environmental engineers
rationale: Environmental engineers do substantial computer-based knowledge work—analyzing data, preparing reports, reviewing permits, and designing pollution-control or water-treatment systems—so AI can meaningfully boost productivity in core parts of the job. But the occupation also depends on site inspections, project monitoring, regulatory accountability, and engineering judgment tied to physical infrastructure, which limits full automation.

title: Umpires, referees, and other sports officials
rationale: This job’s core work happens in person, in real time, on the field or court, requiring physical presence, positioning, visual observation, and split-second judgment under pressure. AI may assist at the margins through video replay, training, and administrative tasks, but it is unlikely to replace the core officiating role broadly in the near term.

title: Bookkeeping, accounting, and auditing clerks
rationale: This job is almost entirely digital, centered on entering, classifying, reconciling, and checking financial data in software, spreadsheets, and databases—exactly the kind of routine information processing AI and automation can increasingly perform. BLS already notes software is reducing demand and shifting remaining workers toward more analytical and advisory tasks, indicating major restructuring even if some human oversight and client communication remain.

title: Sociologists
rationale: Sociologists do predominantly digital knowledge work: designing studies, analyzing data, synthesizing findings, and writing reports and presentations, all of which are areas where AI is advancing quickly. Human judgment remains important for research design, interpreting social context, interviewing people, and policy relevance, but AI can substantially increase productivity and reshape much of the occupation.
